speaker: interviewer
text: September 6, 1995 in Hillside, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, in March, they were taking us from Proszowice into Cracow to a brick factory on the [POLISH]. We stayed in the brick factory for a few months. And during the time, Sundays, we would walk, march into the Cracow ghetto. And we will take a bath, wash our clothes, and I could visit with my uncle Isaac, because he lived in the ghetto. My two younger brothers, Chaim Yosef and Mordechai Yehuda, would take knapsacks with the flours of, of some food. And they would smuggle into the ghetto. They will go by-- there was a small train. They call it a small train. It wasn't a regular train. And that was going on during 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. They will-- that's how they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a cable car.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they could live. In 1942, they start chasing out the people from Proszowice. They start taking people to Treblinka and Majdanek. They forgot about my parents and my brothers and sisters because they were living complete outskirt from Proszowice. Then they found out, and they moved them. They came in with a [NON-ENGLISH]. And they moved them to Koszyce. And they stayed there for the high holidays. People told me that my father was conducting the service. And the praying, the crying to God was no limit. And right after holidays, in November, there was almost Slomniki, Proszowice, Kazimierz, all the towns were Judenrein. So they-- if they find a Jew hidden someplace or whatever-- so they got them all together on the place in Mieroszow. Mieroszow was the biggest city. And in Mieroszow it was maybe 100 people, maybe less. They asked all the men to go to one side-- they knew they're going to be taken to a camp to work, [NON-ENGLISH] camp, arbeitslager-- and the woman and the children to the other side. And when they come for-- to take my family, two brothers, Chaim Yosef and Mordechai Yehuda, and a sister Esther ran away. They ran into the ghetto to Cracow. And my father was left with my mother and two younger children. And when they asked my father to leave to the side to the men, he wouldn't leave. He said I'm not going to leave my wife and my two children. And of course, all the woman and the children, including my father, they shot on the spot. And the other ones, they bought in to Plaszow to the camp. Because in the meantime, I was taken from the brick factory into Plaszow. And when I was taken to Plaszow, at that time Plaszow was only arbeitslager. And our commander was also Miller. And Miller liquidated gulag. And he brought in all, including my brother. And we were both in Plaszow in 1942. And people came in who knew my father, said that they felt guilty that they left their wives and their wives were killed, and the children. And that my father did the right thing. And the irony is that we the ones who were prosecuted felt guilty. And the murders felt good. And this was my two brothers and my sister lived in the ghetto since November '42 till March 13, '43. And March in '43, they liquidated the ghetto. My uncle made a hiding place for his wife and two children. And my older brother and my-- the younger sister was hidden there too with some other people. And my Mordechai Yehuda went out of the ghetto, and he was in Cracow. He was working in the Vistula by people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Vistula?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under water the have the barges. So he worked on the barges as a Christian boy. And he stayed there. The ghetto was liquidated on March 13, on a Saturday. And for whole week, they were bringing out people from the ghetto. There were some in hospital where they shot the-- the people in the hospital. And those who were hidden, those who had no papers, they brought in to Plaszow. And I stayed with the group of workers. Because in Plaszow I was working in [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is building barracks. And we made sidewalks out of the tombstones. And we were taken in to do special work to take off the dead bodies and put them in the hole, and later bury them. In one case, we picked up a little girl, a child, and we walked over-- I hold it in my hand and I walked over to the German. And I asked her if she was 3/4 dead, akt der gnadenschuss. Akt der gnadenschuss means a kindness, a bullet, a kind bullet so the kid will not suffer. And his answer was, for that scheisse, for that piece of shit, it's a schade.

speaker: interviewee
text: Throw it in the grave alive. And that's what we did. And every night after I finish working, my uncle, who was already in Plaszow, because when they liquidated the ghetto, they took him to Plaszow. And he would ask me did you see familiar faces? And every day when I saw little kids, I turned around not to see was my brother, or my cousin, or somebody that I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: A week passed by, and my brother and sister had no more food left. So they tried to get out from-- and it was quiet already in the ghetto. The Germans pulled out. And they went out from the ghetto. They knew how to climb the wall. And they were right on the other side of the wall coming down. And two Polish policemen shot one, one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle left the ghetto when it was liquidated and went to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your brother and sister did not go with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did not. When you've got no [NON-ENGLISH]-- they had no papers to go. And his wife and the two children was also in there. But later, he took them out. But they couldn't stay in the ghetto. My uncle had a factory of the [? Brillos ?], but without the soap. And he was working for the Germans. He delivered to the Germans the [? Brillo, ?] because everything you worked, you gave it to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told Amon Goeth that he has machineries in his factory. His factory was in the ghetto. And from the machineries, he will bring it up to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he put in the two kids. The wife came out regular because she had papers to work in Miedzyrzec. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he put them in the boxes. And he smuggled them into Plaszow. He gave them wet handkerchief so they wouldn't cough and they wouldn't suffocate. And he brought them up to Plaszow. But my brother and sister went a week earlier. And they were shot by-- because they were brought up to Plaszow. And I recognized them. So I was left only with my older brother and my brother in Crakow at the Vistula, at the barges working. Let me go back a little bit. Let me finish off with-- in 1944 in May, Mengele came to Plaszow. And we were on appellplatz. On the place where you, you get together. And I stood with my brother. And we had on our clothing a-- a-- a-- a sign as a Jew and a number. And he took my brother's number down. My brother had something here on his throat. So maybe it caught his eye. And he regi-- signed up all the-- he marked up all the children. And a week later they took him to Auschwitz. And a few days later they took my brother, my two cousins, Esther, my uncle's two children. And we hid in Plaszow from 1943 in March, to 1944 in May, 14 months. And he-- to get food for them, and to carry the stuff out from them. And in May, we almost-- the war was coming to an end. But he couldn't save the two children. And so I was left with the little boy, little brother, who worked on the barges in the Vistula. In October and November, the Vistula froze. So there was no more work. People-- the barges were on a stand still. So my brother had no place to sleep. So he went out in the hills. And there were-- but the shepherds kept a [NON-ENGLISH] and food for the animals. And he slept there. And any time a big snowstorm was through the night, in the morning as he was going down, he make snow steps. And a Polish fellow grabbed him. He said, you must be Jewish. Because if you're not Jewish, you wouldn't be sleeping in, in [NON-ENGLISH]. And he also knew the-- the prayers. He spoke a beautiful Polish. And they took him the police. And they pulled the pants down. And for that the Polish fellow received five pounds of sugar. Somebody told me about it-- but they say they cannot find the Pole. That he run away-- when I was in Cracow after the war. And they killed him. In 1942, when I came to Plaszow, it was, as I said before, Miller. And it wasn't too bad. From Plaszow, we would go every week to the ghetto. I see my uncle. I go to have a bed, and had some food. I will take some stuff with me so I would have it for the week in Plaszow. Until Amon Goeth came. Amon Goeth came to Plaszow either the very end of '42 or beginning of '43. The first thing he did Friday morning when he came in, he assembled us on the appellplatz. He killed the chief of the Jewish police and his assistant. [NON-ENGLISH] was the chief of the police, and Goldberg was his assistant. And that time a Jewish policeman, Chelowicz, who became commander of the camp, I knew him, he lived on [NON-ENGLISH] 6. And we would go to the Vistula. And he would always send me to the store to buy him a couple cigarettes. And he would always tell me, if you gonna hold it strong, and you gonna break the cigarettes, I break your neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was watching, and he was saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. . That means Mother Maria. He's going to kill all of them people. So I went in from the appellplatz. I opened up a window. And I told him run away to the window. And I was working in [NON-ENGLISH]. In [NON-ENGLISH], there were every day shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth, when his maid, a Jewish maid, put down the food for him, he wouldn't eat a lunch or breakfast or dinner if you didn't see Jewish blood. But I knew it from experience, that he had three hats. One hat was like a regular soldier. One was round officer. One was like a Tyrolean hat, a Tyrolean, like a hunting hat. When he put on that Tyrolean hat, he was going after blood. So you had to do-- you couldn't stay and work. Even if you work in barracks, and you did your work, he would walk over. If he feels like not walking another few steps, he would just shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I will run into the latrine. He never walked to the latrine. So in the latrine I stayed till-- I looked through a little hole. I saw him sometimes on a horse, sometimes, mostly by foot, with his big black and white dog. I don't know what's the name of the-- Dalmatian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dalmatian. And because he was afraid maybe somebody going to go after him. Or he will say sometimes to the dog to eat people, to kill people alive. He was a sadist, the worst. And late in '42-- '43, I was working in [NON-ENGLISH]. Matter of fact, I worked where they killed-- they shot the Jewish engineer. And I know the story. There were-- we built in '42, the end of '42, we start the foundation. And something settled. And if she wouldn't be a perfectionist, we could have lifted up and fixed it. But she told the German. And she was on good terms with the German. They worked together not too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: He listens to her. And there was a discussion. He was screaming, mach schnell. You want it fast? And she said no, we have to stop and dig it out. And everything-- there was no machineries. We did everything by hand. And to her, bad luck. Goeth went over, and he said, what is those? What's going on here? So before the German could have said anything to him, he said a Jew is fighting with my-- with German-- how dare you? And he said to her-- said to him, shoot her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wouldn't shoot her. So he gave another order. And he shot her. And two of my friends put her in a wheelbarrow and brought her over to the-- there was a special place, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they throw her in the-- and by the way, whenever they took people from our barracks-- for no reason at all-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], the will always scream at us, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is remember, [NON-ENGLISH] is revenge. And when we heard that they had been shot, we run into the barracks and we started saying a prayer for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because evenings was evening prayer. And the best we could say for ourselves, that they are better off than we are. For them at least we say a prayer, and they are finished with the misery. But for us, who's going to say a prayer for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why to fulfill what they were asking of us, we couldn't take revenge. Jewish people do not take revenge. But [NON-ENGLISH], we must remember. That's why we have to fulfill the obligation. In '43, in August or September, a new administrator by the name of Frank was getting i a villa in Cracow to be the commander for whole Poland. Because Cracow was the hauptstadt, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Capital?

speaker: interviewee
text: The capital. And he wants to show him that he is help-- is working with them. He asked us to load up two truckloads of topsoil. And we didn't know or understand what it is. And 50 boys-- my brother was not there-- they put us on a truck with maybe 20, 10 Ukraine, five Latvian, and five SS. He want to show them that he's protecting the Jews, that they don't run away. And we went to make a garden for that Frank. And the troops, they treat us good over there. They give us food. And we all were mostly were from Cracow. It was near the Cracow stadium. And one boy run into the Cracow stadium, and he run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw him running away, but we wouldn't say nothing. When we got into the camp, they start counting us. And they were screaming, yelling where's the 50th? And we said we didn't know. Nobody will say that they saw him to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't let us into the Plaszow, to the camp. They called on Goeth. And Goeth said, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means kill them all. Then he changed his mind, and he said, every one, second one to be shot. They shot a boy, a friend of mine, to my right hand. Shot a boy to my left hand. We were left either 24 or 25. And he said if once I send you out, and one will escape, I will shoot all of you. This time he was generous, the sadist. And I was starting to be in very big danger in Plaszow. Because when you didn't at your group, they call you out all kind of ways. And I was hungry too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- there was a kitchen in Plaszow that every cook had a kettle. Early in the morning you have to come in to put wood under the kettle and make coffee for the people. Then you had to wash the kettle. And cook some soup for at night. The guys who worked in the kitchen already eated, or had protection, or they were a little older. So I went to the kitchen after I did my day's work. Instead of going to the barracks, I went into the kitchen and I chopped wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a Jewish chef there by the name of Meyer, a German Jew. And he almost-- he fell in love with me. He said, such a little kid-- I really chopped the wood very good. So-- and I told the guys in the kitchen, the cook, that I will wash the kettles for them. So the gave me-- they saved me a little soup. They saved me some bread sometimes. Those things that they couldn't eat. And I used to say to myself-- was the end of '43, and the beginning of '44. And I give some food to my frien-- my brother. And one day Meyer said to me, this camp going to be enlarged. Remember, we're thinking of German-- of the Hungarian Jews to bring, or some other Jews. And when we make a larger kitchen, I'm going to give you a kettle. And to be assigned in the kitchen, you had to go through Oderman, Snyder, Saltz, Goldberg. And of course, it had to come from [NON-ENGLISH]. Some people give maybe some money, or some people had some belongings, some pretty girls. There was no question about it that there was some kind of protection.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one day they enlarged the kitchen. And Meyer said to me, you come to work for me. And Oderman came in before, asking questions. He hit me over the head. And he said, what are you doing here? I said, Meyer-- And he grabbed me by the arm. And he took me to [NON-ENGLISH]. And I told [NON-ENGLISH], well you remember I opened the window for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, in his words-- I'm going to quote them-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This mean a son of a bitch. You son of a bitch. If you telling me you're lying, then I wouldn't have to call the SS to kill you. I'm going to rip you apart. And he took me by the arm again, with Snyder, and to get to that Meyer. And Meyer said, the kid, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the [NON-ENGLISH]-- is saying that you gave him a kettle. You want him to work in the kitchen. He said yes, I want him to work in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, why? What did he give you? And Meyer said, he didn't give me nothing. He's working for months at night chopping wood, washing kettles for-- those guys who I have here, they don't even want to work. I want to give the kid a break. And that's how I got into the kitchen. And that was my survival. In 1944, in [NON-ENGLISH]-- in the meantime-- you want me to talk a little bit about Oskar Schindler? Of course, of course. But before this, is there anything else from Plaszow that, that you want to tell us about Goeth or any other people that you met there that you stayed in contact with?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I worked in the kitchen, there's a lot of people who live and survived that I fed them. I gave out soups. I gave soup. One occasion, Abraham Zukerman-- he's my friend from school. We went to cheder together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw him come in. And we-- and I gave him some soup. And he ate half of the-- you had a little pot. I said, why don't you eat the other one? He said I'm saving this for later. I said come back to the kitchen later and I'll give you more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really I didn't want to make a big deal out of it. But I jeopardize my life. But whoever came in, people that I know, I fed them with some soup. In 1944, Emalia-- let me-- was ordered to-- Schindler to liquidate Emalia. Before that let me go through a little bit about Oskar Schindler. Oskar Schindler was a Sudeten German, who in World War I-- he was born in 1908-- in World War I belonged to Germany, Kaiser Wilhelm. And after World War I, the Czechoslovakia became a state. And he was living in Czechoslovakia. He never liked the Czechs. He-- but he considered himself a German. When the German took over-- annexed Czechoslovakia-- he became a Nazi. And his reward was, when the Germans came into Crakow, they start taking away all the Jewish enterprises, the Jewish stores-- I'm not talking about the little poppa and mama store, but the world wide stores. There were some big stores-- And especially big factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So him, because he spied for the Germans, and he make some friends in the hierarchy in Berlin, he got the manufactory. And in the manufactory, there was a [? Banks, ?] Stern,
